coredata.dc:title: Structural and optical amendment of PVDF into CQDs through high temperature calcination process
coredata.dc:creator: Kaur, Gun Anit
Sharma, Vinit
Gupta, Neeraj
Shandilya, Mamta
Rai, Radheshyam
coredata.dc:description: A controlled and successful high temperature calcination of PVDF was performed to synthesize high quality CQDs from polymeric membrane via electrospinning to control its shape and size. X-Ray Diffractograms show the formation of CQDs after thermal reduction of polymeric nanofibers with an average crystallite size of ~1.9 nm. Raman spectrum reveals the formation of intense G-band which confirms the presence of carbon nanomaterials in the form of CQDs with ratio of intensities IG/ID ~3.7. The variation of FTIR spectrum before and after calcination process supports the speculation of the suppression of oxygen containing functional groups. XPS and UV–Vis absorption spectrum confirms the highly stable and rich carbon content of CC group. FE-SEM and TEM demonstrates the spherical distribution of quantum dots with an average size of ~2.6 nm.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Calcination
Electrospinning
Polymeric nanofiber

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are grateful to Sophisticated Analytical Instrumentation facility (SAIF), Panjab University, Chandigarh for providing characterization facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD diffractograms of CQDs and (b) Raman spectrum of CQDs at room temperature before and after calcination.
(a) Fourier Transform Infrared spectrum, (b) X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, and (c) UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQDs at room temperature.
FE-SEM image of polymeric nanofibrous membrane (a) before and (b) after calcination process, Histograms representing size distribution of (c) nanofibers, and (d) calcined nanofibers.
TEM image at [(a), (b)] low magnification, [(c), (d)] high magnification, and (e) Histogram representing size distribution of CQDs.

head.ce:title: Structural and optical amendment of PVDF into CQDs through high temperature calcination process

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Physics & Materials Science, Shoolini University, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Basic &Applied Sciences, Lingaya's Vidyapeeth, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A controlled and successful high temperature calcination of PVDF was performed to synthesize high quality CQDs from polymeric membrane via electrospinning to control its shape and size. X-Ray Diffractograms show the formation of CQDs after thermal reduction of polymeric nanofibers with an average crystallite size of ~1.9 nm. Raman spectrum reveals the formation of intense G-band which confirms the presence of carbon nanomaterials in the form of CQDs with ratio of intensities IG/ID ~3.7. The variation of FTIR spectrum before and after calcination process supports the speculation of the suppression of oxygen containing functional groups. XPS and UV–Vis absorption spectrum confirms the highly stable and rich carbon content of CC group. FE-SEM and TEM demonstrates the spherical distribution of quantum dots with an average size of ~2.6 nm.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained significant attention by various researchers due to its unique electrical properties caused by quantum confinement and edge effects. Conventional semiconductor QDs based on metallic elements such as CdS, CdSe, PbSe and Ag2S suffer from various problems like toxicity, high cost and hydrophobicity which hinder their practical application. In this context, researchers have shifted their focus towards carbon-based quantum dots due to their high chemical stability, low toxicity, biocompatibility and easy functionalization [1,2]. There are a variety of approaches to synthesize CQDs including pyrolysis, electrochemical exfoliation, incomplete combustion oxidation, laser ablation, acidic oxidation, hydrothermal process, microwave passivation, plasma treatment. However, it is still desirable to synthesize high quality CQDs via a facile and feasible route using low-cost raw materials [3]. Compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots and organic dyes, photoluminescent carbon-based quantum dots are superior in terms of high (aqueous) solubility, robust chemical inertness, facile modification and high resistance to photobleaching. In addition, superior biological properties such as low toxicity and good biocompatibility, makes them a good candidate for potential applications in bioimaging, biosensor and biomolecule/drug delivery. Many approaches have been used to synthesize carbon quantum dots like chemical ablation [4], electrochemical carbonization [5], laser ablation [6], microwave irradiation [7], solvothermal treatment [8]. It was first obtained in 2004 during the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes through preparative electrophoresis and then by using laser ablation of graphite powder and cement in 2006 [9], however this method have several limitations like low quantum yield, and poor control over particle size. The chemical ablation process though is the most accessible and simple method to synthesize carbon quantum dots, however, is done under harsh conditions, yields poor control over size, and uses multiple steps and is therefore a tedious process to commercialize the production of CQDs. Both microwave irradiation and solvothermal treatment again yields poor control over shape, size and physical properties [8]. Therefore, controlling the size of carbon quantum dots is a critical step to reach stable properties for specific applications. Up to now, numerous investigations have been done to attain uniform and homogeneous CQDs through synthesis or post-treatment.

Herein, the main aim of this research work is to synthesize high quality CQDs via electrospinning followed by thermal reduction. Electrospinning is a feasible method to fabricate highly aligned nanofibers under the influence of large electric field [10]. To the best of our knowledge, no research work has been reported to synthesize CQDs using electrospun calcination method.

Poly (vinylidene fluoride)[P(VDF)]; (Mw = 4,75,000 g/mole) pellets (10 wt% solution) were dispersed in a solution of N, N Dimethylformamide/Acetone (3:2 ratio) at 40 °C for 24 h. The solution was fed into the syringe (5 mL) and the metallic needle of the syringe was clipped with the positive terminal of the high voltage power supply (10 kV). The other terminal was clipped to the aluminium foil fixed on the collector plate approximately 15 cm away from the tip of the metallic needle. The rate of flow of solution was set at0.5 mL/h. The polymer solution converted into a nanofibrous webs on the aluminium foil sheet and were calcined at 550 °C for 2 h. The structural properties were studied by XRD(PW3040 Philips X-ray diffractometer) and Raman studies were performed by using Jovin Yvon T64000 micro-Raman microprobe system with Ar ion laser λ = 514.5 nm. The surface morphology was characterized by FE-SEM (Hitachi SU8010 series) and TEM (FEI company of USA). The presence of functional groups was confirmed by FTIR by using Cary630, Agilent Technologies FTIR spectrophotometer and XPS survey spectrum (Thermo fisher scientific). The optical properties were studied using UV–Vis spectroscopy (Perkin-Elmer lambda750).

XRD analysis exhibits two major characteristic peaks of the polymeric nanofiber membrane before calcination process. After calcination process, a broad reflection peak centered at 2θ = 25.3° reveals the graphite like structure of CQDs as shown in Fig. 1 . (a). The broad reflection can be ascribed to the small size of CQDs. The interlayer spacing of CQDs was found to be ~1.8 Å. The average crystallite size (d) was calculated from the line broadening of the recorded XRD data using the Scherrer formula d=
0.89 λ
β c o s θ
, where λ is the wavelength of radiation for Cu Kα, θ is the diffracted angle and βhkl is the Full Width at Half Maximum (FWHM) of diffracted peaks and was found to be ~1.9 nm. The partial degree of crystallization of CQDs can be attributed to the densely packed oxygen containing functional groups on its surface [11] and partial turbostratic carbon structure [12]. Raman spectrum of CQDs reveal the formation of sharp and intense bands as shown in Fig. 1.(b). The main characteristics band observed at 1322 and 1591 cm−1 are D-band and G-band, respectively. The intense G-band as compared to D-band confirms the presence of carbon nanomaterials in the form of CQDs with principally sp2 carbons with some sp3 hybrid carbons. This G-band is also highly sensitive to the strain effects in the sp2 systems. The high ratio of intensities of IG/ID was calculated to be ~3.7 which indicates good-quality graphitic structure [13].

FTIR spectrum displays the significant thermal reduction of nanofibrous webs into CQDs during calcination process as shown in Fig. 2 . (a). There are multiple peaks corresponding to many oxygen containing moieties before calcination process. The vibrational peaks at 795 cm−1, 840 cm−1, 878 cm−1, and 1420 cm-1can be ascribed to the –CH2 rocking vibrations [14] and the deformation vibration of –CH2 groups [15]. The bands at 1182 cm−1 and1270 cm−1 is due to asymmetrical and symmetrical stretching of -CF2 [16]. However, after the calcination process many peaks diminishes substantially. IR bands at 1714 cm-1and 1591 cm-1can be ascribed to the skeletal vibrations of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms in CO group and carboxylic CC group, respectively [17]. A small peak at 2096 cm-1 and 1173 cm-1 is due to –CH group and C-O-C group, respectively [18].

XPS analysis indicates the presence of mainly two elements i.e., C and O as shown in Fig. 2.(b). The deconvolution of C(1 s) spectrum in CQDs show the presence of three peaks discernible at 284.7 eV, 286.6 eV, and 289.7 eV corresponding to the carbon content of CC, C-O, and CO, respectively. The value of atomic percentage of C(1 s) signal for synthesized CQDs was found to be 87.45% which clearly indicates that the surface is highly stable and rich in sp2 C content [19]. UV–Vis absorption spectra of CQDs exhibits two optical absorption peaks at ~217 and ~279 nm as shown in Fig. 2.(c) corresponding to the π-π* transition of the CC bond and the n-π* transition of CO bond [20,21], respectively.

Fig. 3 show FE-SEM image of nanofibrous membrane before and after calcination process. Before calcination process, the polymeric nanofibrous membranes exhibit smooth morphology with an average diameter of the nanofibers ~270 ± 30 nm. A Gaussian fitting curve was used to estimate the average size of FE-SEM and TEM images. After the calcination process, the nanofibers change their morphology and confirms soft agglomeration of CQDs with an average size of ~85 nm.

The morphology and size of the synthesized CQDs were determined by using TEM analysis. TEM images clearly reveal the formation of CQDs with spherical shape and narrow size distributions of about ~2.6 nm as shown in Fig. 4 . The uniform distribution of black dot pattern on the flaks give the preliminary information of CQDs formation as shown in Fig. 4 (a). Moreover, the high resolution analysis clearly shows the formation of CQDs in its grain form as shown in Fig. 4. (b). CQDs consist of many small suborbicular nano-sized dots ranging between 1 and 5 nm. Several small spherical shaped dark dots were detached on the surface homogenously as shown in Fig. 4. (c). Fig. 4.(e) shows the histogram representing the size distribution of CQDs which is in good agreement with the value of average crystallite size from XRD results.

An innovative and facile route was used for sustainable and environment-friendly synthesis of high quality carbon quantum dots (CQDs). CQDs was successfully synthesized by thermal reduction of polymeric nanofibers prepared via electrospinning, for the first time. The combined structural and optical properties confirm the formation of CQDs. A broad reflection in the XRD patterns was ascribed to the formation of small size of CQDs. The interlayer spacing and average crystallite size of CQDs was found to be ~1.8 Å and 1.9 nm, respectively. Raman spectrum also corroborates the formation of CQDs with principally sp2 carbons and some sp3 hybrid carbons. FTIR spectrum confirms the removal of epoxy, and carboxylic groups. Further investigations like XPS and UV–Vis spectrum reveals highly stable and rich sp2 C content surface of CQDs. Furthermore, FE-SEM and TEM images clearly show the formation of quantum dots with an average size of ~85 nm and ~2.6 nm, respectively.

Gun Anit Kaur: Software, Formal analysis. Vinit Sharma: Validation, Writing – original draft. Neeraj Gupta: Conceptualization, Supervision. Mamta Shandilya: Methodology, Investigation. Radheshyam Rai: Project administration, Visualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are grateful to Sophisticated Analytical Instrumentation facility (SAIF), Panjab University, Chandigarh for providing characterization facilities.
